note: Commodity vocabulary. `origins` are region ids (see ports.json regions). `valueTier` 0-5 is coarse relative value per ton (0 = ballast/none). `class:illicit` marks smuggling-prone goods (e.g. tea run past customs). The `enslaved-people` entry is handled specially — see its `framing` block and PLAN §10.5. It is NOT assigned a monetary value tier and NEVER appears as a 'reward'; it exists so the Middle Passage is represented factually rather than effaced.

cargo.name: in ballast (empty)
cargo.note: No cargo; stone/sand for trim. Common on homeward or repositioning legs.

cargo.name: salt
cargo.note: Setúbal/Bay salt for the fisheries and Baltic.

cargo.name: naval stores
cargo.note: Tar, pitch, hemp, cordage.

cargo.name: bar iron
cargo.note: Swedish oregrounds iron, prized for steel.

cargo.name: wine & brandy
cargo.note: Claret, port, sherry, French brandy.

cargo.name: sugar
cargo.note: The great plantation staple of the Atlantic.

cargo.name: coffee
cargo.note: Mocha first — the Red Sea trade supplied Europe before Java and the islands.

cargo.name: tea
cargo.note: The bulk of the China trade; heavily smuggled before the 1784 Commutation Act.

cargo.name: porcelain
cargo.note: Also 'chinaware'; often shipped as profitable ballast under tea.

cargo.name: spices
cargo.note: Nutmeg, mace, cloves, pepper — the VOC monopoly staples.

cargo.name: Indian textiles
cargo.note: Calico, chintz, muslin — a principal Indian-Ocean export.

cargo.name: specie (silver & gold)
cargo.note: Bullion/coin; the outbound counterpart to the China & India trade, and Brazilian gold.

cargo.name: trade goods for barter
cargo.note: The outbound Guinea cargo bartered on the African coast: textiles, firearms, gunpowder, brandy, iron bars, cowrie shells, manillas. Its exchange for captive people is the transaction the Middle Passage rests on.

cargo.name: black pepper
cargo.note: Malabar pepper — the commodity that first drew Europe east.

cargo.name: cloves
cargo.note: Moluccan monopoly staple; smuggled through Makassar against the VOC.

cargo.name: nutmeg & mace
cargo.note: Banda Islands — the world's only source until the 1770s.

cargo.name: cinnamon
cargo.note: Ceylon; a VOC monopoly from Galle after 1640.

cargo.name: saltpetre
cargo.note: Bengal saltpetre for Europe's powder mills; favourite Indiaman kentledge.

cargo.name: cowrie shells
cargo.note: Maldivian shell money, shipped via Bengal to Europe and on to West Africa as slave-trade currency.

cargo.name: pearls
cargo.note: Bahrain and Kharg banks — the Gulf's ancient luxury fishery.

cargo.name: frankincense & dates
cargo.note: Omani coastal staples, carried with the monsoon.

cargo.name: ivory
cargo.note: Swahili-coast and Guinea ivory for Surat and Europe.

cargo.name: gold dust
cargo.note: The Gold Coast trade that named Elmina ('the mine').

cargo.name: gum arabic
cargo.note: Senegal river trade; indispensable to European textile printing.

cargo.name: palm oil
cargo.note: The Bight of Biafra's 'legitimate trade', rising as abolition closes the slave trade.

cargo.name: furs
cargo.note: Russian sables out of Arkhangelsk; sea-otter pelts from Sitka for the Canton market.

cargo.name: whale oil & baleen
cargo.note: Spitsbergen fishery — Smeerenburg's 'blubber town'.

cargo.name: dried salt cod
cargo.note: The Grand Banks fishery; Louisbourg's staple and Catholic Europe's protein.

cargo.name: Japanese copper
cargo.note: Dejima's principal export after silver was banned — VOC bar copper for India's mints.

cargo.name: ginseng & medicines
cargo.note: Korean ginseng through Busan's Japan House, traded against Japanese silver.

cargo.name: deerskins & sappanwood
cargo.note: Ayutthaya's staples for the Japan trade.

cargo.name: enslaved people

framing.label: captive people held below decks — the Middle Passage
framing.description: Human beings trafficked against their will across the Atlantic. Carried only on the middle leg of the triangular trade, from a West African embarkation port to a plantation colony. This is recorded, not celebrated: no monetary value is assigned, mortality risk is elevated to reflect the documented lethality of the passage, and it is never framed as profit or reward.
framing.source: SlaveVoyages — Trans-Atlantic Slave Trade Database (voyage durations, embarkation counts, mortality).
framing.mortalityNote: Recorded shipboard mortality on the Middle Passage commonly ran ~10-20% and at times far higher; the sim's loss/attrition model reflects this rather than hiding it.

cargo.note: Assigned ONLY to whydah->kingston / whydah->bahia legs by routes.json. build-data.mjs asserts this cargo never appears on any other lane.
